questions.question: What does 'human-in-the-loop' mean for production agents?
options: A human must write every prompt manually
The agent cannot run without a human watching the console
The agent pauses and asks for human approval before taking certain actions
Humans review agent outputs after the fact, not in real time
questions.explanation: Human-in-the-loop means the agent checkpoints at critical decision points — like sending an email or deleting a file — and waits for explicit human confirmation before proceeding.

questions.question: Why should you implement rate limiting for agents?
options: To prevent runaway agents from making thousands of costly API calls
To make the agent respond more slowly for a better UX
To comply with GDPR data regulations
To ensure the agent uses only one tool at a time
questions.explanation: Agents in loops can spiral into making rapid-fire API calls if something goes wrong. Rate limiting caps how many calls can be made per unit of time, protecting both cost and downstream services.

questions.question: Sandboxing an agent's tools means:
options: Running the agent on a dedicated GPU server
Only allowing the agent to use one tool at a time
Restricting what the tools can access or modify in the environment
questions.explanation: Sandboxing limits the blast radius of an agent's actions — for example, a code execution tool might run in a Docker container so it cannot affect the host filesystem.
